Hello everyone, our Gold Plus members. This is Bill Glazer and on the call with me today is Dan Kennedy as usual and also with us today is our guest expert Joe Sugarman who everyone I think on this call should certainly know of Joe. He's clearly one of the nation's top copywriters. He has sold millions and millions and millions of dollars using his pen to sell products. He's best known as being the creator, originator of Blue Block or Sunglasses which he's used a variety of ways to sell that product very successfully including infomercials which it's hard. You probably have to be living in a cave not to have seen at least one of those. He is credited with being the first person to actually use 800 numbers in order to take orders. So he was the inventor of that. He's written a fabulous book which is called Triggers, 30 sales tools you can use to control the mind and your prospects to motivate, influence, and persuade. So welcome Joe and welcome Dan. Well thank you. It's a really pleasure to be on the phone call. Thanks. I'm going to get right to the meat of our topic today which is Triggers. Joe let me just first ask you what do you mean when you refer to these triggers? What is your definition of them? Well a psychological trigger is some, oh psychological thing that suddenly causes you to say yes to a sales message. In other words sometimes it's a sudden thing, sometimes it's very subtle but it affects you in such a way that it convinces you to buy something at that, when you use that particular trigger. A psychological trigger is something that causes an emotional reaction and causes you to make a decision. I think maybe that's the keyword, just make a decision. Well as I mentioned you have 30 different of these psychological triggers that you identify in your book. We could probably spend our entire 90 minute call just talking about each of them. So this could really be 90, it could actually be 30 calls. But what I'd like to try to do today is to identify some of them that are in your book and to spend some time on a few of them. We're not going to get through all 30 of them today for sure. But the first one I'd like to talk about which is something that I'm always thinking about when I'm writing copy and I know that Dan does the same thing is one of your triggers which you identify is objection raising. Well first of all what do you mean by objection raising and how do you usually employ that in your copy? Well if you're selling a product and there is an issue about the product that might cause a consumer to say well I don't know if I want to buy this product because it's got this problem or it's got this it's got too high a price. If you don't raise that objection yourself in your sales presentation you're not going to you're not going to identify with that consumer. Excuse me he's still going to have that feeling or she's going to still have that feeling that you're really you're not really resolving the objection that that's in the back of his or her mind. A good example would be let's talk about a high price. Let's say you're selling something that's a lot more expensive than another product and you're trying to sell it and you give all the great features. If you would say something like now we know this is more expensive and but the reason it's more expensive is because and then you list all the reasons you've resolved that objection. You've kind of eliminated that that concern by that person or at least alleviated it to such an extent where they feel more inclined to purchase. And so I give excuse me I give an example in my book of when all these DC-10 plane crashes took place this was back in the late 70s so I think it was 1979. McDonnell Douglas who manufactured that plane came out with an ad written by an astronaut saying that the plane was the DC-10 is one of the safest and one of the best planes on the in the air and that I have personally flown them and I find it to be a very safe plane and all this kind of stuff. But if I was writing that same ad I would have said you probably heard about all these plane crashes involving the DC-10. Well let me tell you something it's really a very safe plane and let me tell you why and then you listen in other words you raise the objection that people have in their minds in the back of their minds and you you resolve it and if you don't do that those people are still going to have that objection in the back of their mind you've got to you've got to bring it out and you've got to resolve it at the same time. McDonnell Douglas we know Dan one of the things I've heard you say often is that prior to writing sing-down and writing a sales message you like to make the list of all the reasons why someone they might not buy. How do you go about identifying those and then what are some of the ways that you then overcome that in your copy. Well I do make that list more often than not you're right I think it's a good structural tool and even in some cases either governs the way you write the main sales letter or if we're talking about print or some corollary to it like a question and answer piece that's enclosed and and if you're using other media like both Joe and I've done a work with Longform TV the list is really really important and typically we'll do them then on file cards and storyboard it and rearrange them and rearrange them and literally the order in which we're going to address and make the objections go away will sometimes control the entire the entire script of the show as to how you figure them out I mean in many cases a product or a service or a proposition has some pretty obvious flaws just about nothing any of us are ever going to sell is perfect and most people really whether they're selling by media or even when they're selling face to face function out of fear rather than courage and so they they're well aware of what the 500 pound pink elephant is over in the corner of the room they're just hoping that the prospect doesn't notice it and and of course that's you know a feudal hope in fact sort of the the one point I did want to make that is if you will triggered by this discussion is that pretty much everything in business boils down to a to a continuum of either fear or courage and just about every business decision made from the fear side rather than the courage side turns out badly some of the courage ones turned out badly too but just about all the ones made out of fear turn out badly and just about everything you do in in advertising marketing or sales based upon fear you know is is is not as effective as it could be otherwise and Joe's example of the of the troubled airplane is you know an excellent one and I would totally concur you'd be far better off beginning your advertising and sales message acknowledging that elephant that hey we all know these things have been falling out of the sky now now now let's go from there yeah exactly you know rather than having the message ignored because you didn't say it you know in the other ways you find the objections one good way is either you sell the thing face to face nose to nose toes to toes or you get a transcript of a presentation made by the company sales best sales people or you won't play it with people who are encouraged to raise every known obstacle and objection to the presentation I imagine with blue blockers I would speculate long before you shot the first show you figured out a way to do that pitch in some kind of an environment where you would discover people's negative reactions as well as their positive reactions yeah well and and we captured some of those we actually looked for them we actually had so few that whatever few we had we put in just to make it real make it real again well I was that was probably a good point you know a really good example that I mentioned in my book triggers is the thermostat we offered and it was an ugly looking thermostat it had a funny display it required installation it had all these things that I would object to if I were personally buying that in other words it was my personal common sense look at this product that convinced me this is what it was an ugly product and a very difficult one to sell so what I did was I raised the objections in the beginning I said hey look at you we were presented this this thermostat and it was ugly looking it was old technology I mean we're selling all these space age products this thing looked like it was 20 years old and I went on and I said and and plus it it it didn't there was no buttons or switches you can press to increase or decrease the time and I go into this whole thing and then I also at the very end mentioned the fact that it required installation and who wants to mess around with all these electrical wires and that's how I started the ad and people reading that ad which is so well what what's this guy gonna do he's simply knocking this product so they keep reading and then I said you know but I noticed one thing it said that it's really simple to install and the manufacturer urged me to just simply install it just see what it's like and I and because the red wire would go to where they're only 24 volts so you don't have to worry about high voltage and the red wire goes to the red wire in the in your wall and and the black wire goes to the black wire and so it's really easy to install so I installed it and guess what I think this thing is the greatest product I've ever tried and and and seen in my life here's why and then I went into all the details it did use an integrated circuit and it had all these great features but the point was that I raised all of these objections I raised all the objections that I personally had and the big one was the installation and of course I covered that as well and so I raised all those objections I resolved them with that thermostat ad and that ad ran for three years until a company that sold us the product was acquired by Honeywell and we made it a brand name actually by advertising in print for three solid years people really became familiar with it and I you know I think the point that's important is that had you not raised and overcome the objections the success of selling the product would have been nowhere near what you achieved I'd like to just make one other point about these objections before we move on to the next trigger which is that I have found that when I use testimonials either in print or audio testimonials that there's two main categories of testimonials that I often use one of them one of them is a category which the testimonial gives a specific outcome by using the product or service that I'm selling and the second testimonial that I find very effective is when the testimonial talks about overcoming an objection that's going on in the prospects mind and if so when a testimonial actually says gee you know I was skeptical at first also and I thought that the plane was going to fall out of the sky but then goes on and says but here's been my experience that's another really great use of testimonials and in overcoming objections yeah good point well let's move on which is to the next trigger I like to discuss which is one of my favorite ones and I find that most people are and I don't see people doing this very well or often enough which is this whole concept of involvement and ownership and Joe could you first identify or define what you mean by this and then we could talk about a little bit well I tell a story in my my book about the salesman who used to sell television sets before the remote control and he had the best sales record in his his store and all the other salesmen wondered how did this guy do it how did he sell more than anybody else he didn't seem in that spectacular and so they so finally on his retirement he got up and gave his little retirement speech he says I'm going to tell you the secret he says I stand in the beginning of the store and I watch people go in and if I see somebody turning the knobs of those tv sets changing the channels or adjusting something I get I walk up to them right away because I know that I have a 50 percent chance of selling them but if I walk up to somebody who hasn't touched a knob hasn't done anything chances are maybe I've got a 10 percent chance and and that's that's also true in direct marketing and Dan you probably know this and Bill you I'm sure you've seen these things where they come in the mail it said that says if you want this free subscription take out this tab and put it in the yes box and if you don't put it in the no box or something like that you know some juvenile approach and you look at that and you say who they hell why the hell they put that in what do they think I'm a juvenile you know an idiot yet they found that this type of involvement device doubled response it doubled it does and double it in some cases tripled it and so I learned very early that and when you involve somebody in a in the product in a product or a process they are more likely to buy for example when we would sell them let's say a pocket calculator this was many years ago we would say pick up the pocket calculator feel the keys notice when you know when you get the pocket calculator notice how they click instead of just you know you just push them and and look at the large display and see how easy it is to read the digits and we'd go into this as if the person was actually involved with that product and that that involvement works very well now it's especially relevant in on the internet when you have an internet site and you cause people to click here and do this and do that the more they get involved with your site and the more they click around and navigate the more involved they become and the more the greater the chance of you selling them something well you know I think you're earlier observation of you know that you don't see it done much and you don't see it done well I think the reason for that is is that it it slows the copywriting job up considerably and a lot of people do most of their ads and their direct mail pieces in a in a blinding hurry this particular technique takes a little thought and time if you're going to if you're going to construct one of those question and answer quizzes where they're checking off boxes that is designed to lead them to the decision or to some scoring mechanism that makes them woefully inadequate and in desperate need of what it is that you offer I mean it takes some time and thought to construct one of those things properly if you're going to write copy like Joe described for the calculator which um talking about selling electronic calculators as if they are new technology and using the phrase many years ago seemed somewhat inadequate it's actually prehistoric and maybe the only three people on the planet who could remember having bought the our first big clumsy giant craig calculators for 89 or 99 dollars but try 249 yeah I think the first one I bought was 99 bucks if I'm not mistaken but then no I was more in the joke category of good 49 but um you know that that copy where you're actually putting them in the experience and trying to make them feel you know a part of something that is occurring in real and present time um it is it's fairly difficult to uh to uh do and um so a lot of it doesn't get done you know for the same reason that down at the small business level there's somebody bad yellow pages ads because the guy waits to do the ad until the yellow page rep shows up and says I gotta have it tomorrow um and consequently um this this this was a really good one to talk about because if you're looking for leverage if you're looking for competitive differential if you happen to be mailing to customers who get a lot of direct mail in your product category or you're trying to in copyright terms be to control um this this is a very worthwhile strategy yeah you know Dan there's one other example that I uh given my book also and that was we had a I mean you talked about involvement device we were going to sell a a uh a spell spelling computer this was of course before the spell checker on the computers it was a spell checker it was a little handheld thing that you enter the word as close as you can and it gave you all of possible spelling similar to a dictionary and um we were and we were offered this product but we thought it was a lot it was way too expensive but we came up with an idea and that is we ran an ad in the wall street journal and we and in the ad we had 20 spelling mistakes we said to people look at this ad contains 20 spelling mistakes if you can find all this whatever we said if you for every spelling mistake you find we'll give you two dollars off this product and um and and that was really an involvement device as a matter of fact I got a call from one of my dear friends who said Joe I see says I normally spend about a half hour reading the wall street journal every day I've spent an hour and a half just reading your damn ad trying to find out all the spelling errors now would you tell me over the phone so I what am I missing we even misspelled the word misspelled uh I mean and and uh what we found and this was really fun a lot of people it was a very successful ad by the way and what we found was that a lot of people didn't catch all 20 misspelled words maybe they caught 10 and we made a lot more money as a result in fact our projections we beat our projections so that was a beautiful example of an involvement device you get them involved you get them emotionally involved physically involved well let's move on um only because of time constraints and trying to get through as many as possible the next one I want to talk about is is um a trigger that that that you know both you Joe and Dan are are masters at and as a matter of fact um you know what Joe you've been giving some examples of this as you've been talking which is this uh storytelling in your book in your book triggers you actually illustrate uh all of your triggers by doing storytelling and telling you specific examples of how you've employed them um I think that you know like the last one if you're looking for a big competitive advantage this is a place to get it um Joe let me just sort of turn it over to you and I'd like to hear your comments about it well storytelling is a very powerful powerful tool to use in any form of selling I talk about it in indirect marketing um we as as humans we grew up having stories read to us that's how we discovered the outside world our parents either read books to us or even tv we've watched tv and we were told stories stories uh just grab you and uh if you can tell a story that involves your product and that uh points out certain features of your product you're going to grab the consumer you know I always use this as an example sometimes I'm at a speech and the speaker will get up there and it'll be a really boring speaker but the minute the speaker says let me tell you a story boy I wake up it was just like a natural reaction and I start listening to that what a story does and especially when used in the very beginning in fact it should really be used at the very beginning is it draws the the the the listener or the reader or the person into the copy and it's been proven that once you get a person to read let's say the first two or three paragraphs of your of your ad copy they're going to read the whole thing uh it's like a locomotive once you get started they're going to read the whole thing and um I give an example in my book of a blue blocker when blue blocker started out I mean that that became a major corporation we sold 20 million pairs of sunglasses over a period of well we're now in our 18th year we're still selling them we're very active in that but but our the copy started out as something like I'm about to tell you a true story if you believe me you will be well rewarded if you don't believe me I will make it worth your while to change your mind let me explain and then I go into the story I tell about how this friend of mine presented a pair of sunglasses and I he said that it would be really it's incredible way to look through them and and and that's how I started that ad and of course that ad led to a major corporation and also an infomercial which which we ran different infomercials for the same product over a period of six years and it all started with a story stories are extremely important and very valuable if you can come up with a story in the beginning of your advertising copy to get people into the text then you have got a very powerful tool I think I think what should be interesting to all of our members who are listening to this is that you know this storytelling is so applicable in just about any type of format or media that you're using not not just you know in sales letters and not just I mean you would mention when you make up a speech that you are incorporating stories and I know Dan you do the same I mean I do also I guess Dan where's some of the other formats that you find this most effective well you know Joe's absolutely right I mean we've been we've been conditioned since early childhood when the deepest grooves in the in the subconscious are formed to to want to hear stories to want to be told stories and so you know pretty much every major religion as well as secular mysticism cults movements you know they're all built around charismatic individuals telling stories and parables and so it's obviously the right way to sell I think the trick is and the mistakes that get made mostly whether you're doing it in print or one-on-one or from the platform is is to make sure that you that you've chosen and constructed a very relevant story that you're not telling a story that actually detracts from the sale or puts a bump in the greased shoot that slows somebody up and they kind of get lost in the story they can't be any longer than absolutely necessary and so you know a lot of people have sort of favorite stories that they like to tell that have nothing to do with what they're selling yeah or the story keeps getting embellished and embellished and embellished and longer and longer and longer and the thing takes on this this horrid life of its own sucking up more and more time and space and so you have to be really really careful about that but but there's no doubt that they're very very powerful you know Dan if I can interrupt you for a second you remember the story of the Batman credit card absolutely I mean I tell that at many of my speeches and Dan is involved in this too he gave me the final comment in my little story but anyway to make a long story short when I get up to give a talk people expect me to get up there and talk about how successful I've been and how to make a lot of money and instead I get up there and I talk about this Batman credit card that I once came out with back in 1966 and try to make my first million and how I just failed miserably at it and ended up with a quarter of a million of them and could not sell them and tried year after year and I tell all the different ways I tried through the years 30 years go by I haven't been able to sell it and then I tell the story about Dan walking up to me at a seminar and he said to me Joe he said you remember that Batman credit card I said yeah and he said well you gave one to a friend of mine and he gave it to his son and his son put it on eBay and he sold it for $400 and that's usually how I culminate my talk and that's story and I went and I when I tell it to an audience I mean you could hear you can see that that audience is there's not there's nobody moving or twitching I mean they're listening to every word I say because it's like an adventure it's a story and that gets him into the into the into the rest of the talk in which I share very important points although I even share them during that story because they do bring out certain points that the audience is interested in hearing so yeah that's a another good example but I wanted to mention that then because I'll never forget that that was and I use that in my speeches all the time nice to have been helpful and as I said off the call I'm sure you wish that however many of those darn things you still have left gathering dust somewhere you could sell each one of them for but I'd be yeah 10 million dollars worth yeah well um let's move on I mean I have a long list of triggers here but uh we're never going to get to all of them but I'd like to move on to uh well let's move on to one other one that I use a lot which is uh specificity um and Joe I'm gonna let you identify it if you give an example have you used it and then I'd be more than happy to tell a little bit about my experience well I in my book I talk about uh the fact that when you use a specific number to identify a um oh or anything you're talking about you come across as being more credible uh for example if you said something like dentists everywhere uh use such and such a toothpaste you sound like typical advertising babble but if you said something like in a recent survey 92 percent of the dentist actually used the toothpaste it sounds more credible uh if you talk about 72 000 nerve endings at the bottom of your feet uh you sound more knowledgeable you sound like you really know your subject you sound like an expert and that's really important because people love dealing with experts so the more specific you are the more credible your presentation is you want to get as far away from this typical advertising babble and get into the specifics of a particular product and and give specific figures as well and that's what we mean by specific I can hardly pronounce this occasionally but would you pronounce it for me uh specific and I'm a guy that's your little mumbles his words you're asking the wrong guy for help I should never use that word because I can never pronounce it um yeah let me just um add to that that you know in when people give offers um the most two most common ways that people give offers are usually either percentage discount or dollar discount and I've tested this in my own retail stores and we've tested um a 20 percent discount and we've tested a 21.7 percent discount and we've tested a 19.3 percent discount and we have found that the best reason the highest responding discount is 19.3 percent which is interesting because it's lower in the discount than the other two that we tested and but but I think what the reason why is because of this issue of specificity where when you specify a specific number and in this particular case I'm a big believer in using very unusual odd numbers because I think unusual odd numbers even get more attention given to them you know so once again giving your example of the 72,000 nerve endings you know I think 72,113 nerve endings is even better but the more specific that you can be I think you come off as being more credible and more people notice it I know Dan I know you've been teaching this as well for years well the classic example of this when I first started speaking in 1978 um I was doing a awards banquet for a real estate company and to show you how dumb I was I mean they were given an award to their top performer that year who had sold a home every single week and I said to the sales manager next to me I said gee it doesn't seem like much to me because my frame of reference was a sale a day and and so I thought she was underperforming and so if you use a statistic and you don't refer and you don't reference frame it for the reader or the audience and you assume they know what you know you can have done the right thing and still gotten a bad result so I'm all about meaningful specifics and making sure that you wrap them in a frame of reference so so your reader or the audience really gets it
